contributor.username: ja>埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会

revision.comment: 埼玉県護国神社
revision.text:
'''埼玉縣護國神社'''（埼玉県護国神社）は、埼玉県さいたま市大宮区に鎮座する護国神社です。 鳥羽伏見の役以後の國事に殉じた埼玉県関係の英霊5万1千余柱を祀る。
{{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]高鼻町3丁目149番地 |祭神 = [[埼玉県出身関係殉国の英霊]] |創建 = [[1934年4月]]（[[昭和]]9年） }}

contributor.username: ja>埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会

revision.comment: 埼玉県護国神社
revision.text:
'''埼玉縣護國神社'''（埼玉県護国神社）は、埼玉県さいたま市大宮区に鎮座する護国神社です。
鳥羽伏見の役以後の國事に殉じた埼玉県関係の英霊5万1千余柱を祀る。 {{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]高鼻町3丁目149番地 |祭神 = [[埼玉県出身関係殉国の英霊]] |創建 = [[1934年4月]]（[[昭和]]9年） }}

revision.comment: 加筆
画像のアップロード
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:埼玉県護国神社鳥居.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]高鼻町3丁目149番地 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.0|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の英霊 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[指定護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 祭神 == [[File:埼玉県護国神社拝殿.jpg|thumb|本殿]] [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の[[英霊]]51,000余[[柱]]を祀る。 == 由緒 == [[File:埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔.jpg|thumb|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔
第58代厚生大臣　渡辺美智雄　書]] [[昭和]]以前に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたところ、昭和の初めに[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まったため、同9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に「埼玉県招魂社」として創祀、同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となった。[[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。 == 外部リンク== *[http://gokoku.info/ 埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会] {{DEFAULTSORT:さいたまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:さいたま市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:大宮区]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]高鼻町3丁目149番地 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.0|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の英霊 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[指定護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 祭神 == [[File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|thumb|本殿]] [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の[[英霊]]51,000余[[柱]]を祀る。 == 由緒 == [[File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|thumb|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔
第58代厚生大臣　渡辺美智雄　書]] [[昭和]]以前に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたところ、昭和の初めに[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まったため、同9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に「埼玉県招魂社」として創祀、同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となった。[[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。 == 外部リンク== *[http://gokoku.info/ 埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会] {{DEFAULTSORT:さいたまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:さいたま市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:大宮区]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]

鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]高鼻町3丁目149番地 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 55 |緯度秒 = 10.6 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 37 |経度秒 = 40.3 |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の英霊 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] |地図 = Japan Saitama }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[指定護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 祭神 == [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の[[英霊]]51,000余[[柱]]を祀る。 == 由緒 == [[昭和]]以前に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたところ、昭和の初めに[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まったため、同9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に「埼玉県招魂社」として創祀、同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となった。[[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。 また、平成25年10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|本殿 File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:03 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]高鼻町3丁目149番地 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 55 |緯度秒 = 10.6 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 37 |経度秒 = 40.3 |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] |地図 = Japan Saitama |ラベル位置 = left }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[指定護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 由緒・歴史 == 埼玉県人の英霊の祭祀は、[[昭和]]以前は県内各所に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたが、[[満州事変]]で県下の英霊が増えたのを機に、[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まり、昭和9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に[[朝香宮鳩彦王]]（当時[[近衛師団長]]）臨席の下、「埼玉県招魂社」として鎮座した。同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となる。 [[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。昭和30年（1955年）、奉賛会が設立される。 また、平成25年10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の戦没者51180[[柱]]を祀る。 == 祭事 == * 1月1日 - 元旦祭 * 2月11日 - 紀元祭 * 2月17日 - 祈年祭 * 春分の日 - 春分祭 * 4月9日 - 例大祭 * 5月5日 - 祝子祭 * 8月15日 - みたま祭り * 秋分の日 - 秋分祭 * 11月3日 - 文化祭 * 11月23日 - 新嘗祭・勤労感謝祭 * 12月23日 - 天長祭 * 12月31日 - 除夜祭 * 毎月1日 - 月次祭 == ギャラリー == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|本殿 File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:03 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]高鼻町3丁目149番地 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 55 |緯度秒 = 10.6 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 37 |経度秒 = 40.3 |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] |地図 = Japan Saitama |ラベル位置 = left }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[指定護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 由緒・歴史 == 埼玉県人の英霊の祭祀は、[[昭和]]以前は県内各所に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたが、[[満州事変]]で県下の英霊が増えたのを機に、[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まり、昭和9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に[[朝香宮鳩彦王]]（当時[[近衛師団長]]）臨席の下、「埼玉県招魂社」として鎮座した。同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となる。 [[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。昭和30年（1955年）、奉賛会が設立される。 また、平成25年10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の戦没者51180[[柱]]を祀る。 == 祭事 == * 1月1日 - 元旦祭 * 2月11日 - 紀元祭 * 2月17日 - 祈年祭 * 春分の日 - 春分祭 * 4月9日 - 例大祭 * 5月5日 - 祝子祭 * 8月15日 - みたま祭り * 秋分の日 - 秋分祭 * 11月3日 - 文化祭 * 11月23日 - 新嘗祭・勤労感謝祭 * 12月23日 - 天長祭 * 12月31日 - 除夜祭 * 毎月1日 - 月次祭 == ギャラリー == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|本殿 File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔
第58代厚生大臣　渡辺美智雄　書 == 外部リンク== *[http://gokoku.info/ 埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会] {{DEFAULTSORT:さいたまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:埼玉県の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:大宮区の歴史]] [[Category:大宮区の建築物]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:03 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]][[高鼻町]]3丁目149番地 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 55 |緯度秒 = 10.6 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 37 |経度秒 = 40.3 |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] |地図 = Japan Saitama |ラベル位置 = left }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 由緒・歴史 == 埼玉県人の英霊の祭祀は、[[昭和]]以前は県内各所に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたが、[[満洲事変]]で県下の英霊が増えたのを機に、[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まり、昭和9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に[[朝香宮鳩彦王]]（当時[[近衛師団長]]）臨席の下、「埼玉県招魂社」として鎮座した。同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となる。 [[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。昭和30年（1955年）、奉賛会が設立される。 また、平成25年10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の戦没者51180[[柱]]を祀る。 == 祭事 == * 1月1日 - 元旦祭 * 2月11日 - 紀元祭 * 2月17日 - 祈年祭 * 春分の日 - 春分祭 * 4月9日 - 例大祭 * 5月5日 - 祝子祭 * 8月15日 - みたま祭り * 秋分の日 - 秋分祭 * 11月3日 - 文化祭 * 11月23日 - 新嘗祭・勤労感謝祭 * 12月23日 - 天長祭 * 12月31日 - 除夜祭 * 毎月1日 - 月次祭 == ギャラリー == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|本殿 File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔
第58代[[厚生省|厚生大臣]]　[[渡辺美智雄]]　書 == 外部リンク== *[https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ 埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会] {{DEFAULTSORT:さいたまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:さいたま市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:大宮区の歴史]] [[Category:大宮区の建築物]] {{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:03 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]][[高鼻町]]3丁目149番地 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 55 |緯度秒 = 10.6 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 37 |経度秒 = 40.3 |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] |地図 = Japan Saitama |ラベル位置 = left }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 由緒・歴史 == 埼玉県人の英霊の祭祀は、[[昭和]]以前は県内各所に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたが、[[満洲事変]]で県下の英霊が増えたのを機に、[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まり、昭和9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に[[朝香宮鳩彦王]]（当時[[近衛師団長]]）臨席の下、「埼玉県招魂社」として鎮座した。社殿の設計は[[二本松孝蔵]]。同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となる。 [[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。昭和30年（1955年）、奉賛会が設立される。 [[1967年]]（昭和42年）[[10月23日]]、[[昭和天皇]]、[[香淳皇后]]が[[第22回国民体育大会]]に合わせて県内を行幸啓。護国神社が訪問先の一つとなる{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=131 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。 また、平成25年10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の戦没者51180[[柱]]を祀る。 == 祭事 == * 1月1日 - 元旦祭 * 2月11日 - 紀元祭 * 2月17日 - 祈年祭 * 春分の日 - 春分祭 * 4月9日 - 例大祭 * 5月5日 - 祝子祭 * 8月15日 - みたま祭り * 秋分の日 - 秋分祭 * 11月3日 - 文化祭 * 11月23日 - 新嘗祭・勤労感謝祭 * 12月23日 - 天長祭 * 12月31日 - 除夜祭 * 毎月1日 - 月次祭 == ギャラリー == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|本殿 File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:03 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]][[高鼻町]]3丁目149番地 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 55 |緯度秒 = 10.6 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 37 |経度秒 = 40.3 |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] |地図 = Japan Saitama |ラベル位置 = left }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 由緒・歴史 == 埼玉県人の英霊の祭祀は、[[昭和]]以前は県内各所に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたが、[[満洲事変]]で県下の英霊が増えたのを機に、[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まり、昭和9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に[[朝香宮鳩彦王]]（当時[[近衛師団長]]）臨席の下、「埼玉県招魂社」として鎮座した。社殿の設計は[[二本松孝蔵]]。同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となる。 [[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。昭和30年（1955年）、奉賛会が設立される。 [[1967年]]（昭和42年）[[10月23日]]、[[昭和天皇]]、[[香淳皇后]]が[[第22回国民体育大会]]に合わせて県内を行幸啓。護国神社が訪問先の一つとなる{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=131 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。 また、平成25年10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の戦没者51180[[柱]]を祀る。 == 祭事 == * 1月1日 - 元旦祭 * 2月11日 - 紀元祭 * 2月17日 - 祈年祭 * 春分の日 - 春分祭 * 4月9日 - 例大祭 * 5月5日 - 祝子祭 * 8月15日 - みたま祭り * 秋分の日 - 秋分祭 * 11月3日 - 文化祭 * 11月23日 - 新嘗祭・勤労感謝祭 * 12月23日 - 天長祭 * 12月31日 - 除夜祭 * 毎月1日 - 月次祭 == ギャラリー == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|本殿 File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔
第58代[[厚生省|厚生大臣]]　[[渡辺美智雄]]　書 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク== *[https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ 埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会] {{DEFAULTSORT:さいたまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:さいたま市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:大宮区の歴史]] [[Category:大宮区の建築物]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{draft}} {{afc comment|1=Is this even a Gokoku shrine I am not sure[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 11:28, 12 December 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tanabio Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[ja:棚尾神社]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Original pages]]{{gokoku shrines}}[[category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} {{Shrine infobox | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and Main Hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | address = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (Annual Festival) }} '''Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. It enshrines the spirits of war dead from Saitama Prefecture and was previously designated as a "specified Gokoku Shrine." == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports Festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == Annual Festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day Festival * February 17 – Prayer Festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox Festival * April 9 – Annual Grand Festival * May 5 – Children's Day Festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (Festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox Festival * November 3 – Culture Day Festival * November 23 – Niiname Festival & Labor Thanksgiving Festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday Festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve Festival * Monthly – Monthly Festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Main Hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Original pages]]{{gokoku shrines}}[[category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports Festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == Annual Festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day Festival * February 17 – Prayer Festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox Festival * April 9 – Annual Grand Festival * May 5 – Children's Day Festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (Festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox Festival * November 3 – Culture Day Festival * November 23 – Niiname Festival & Labor Thanksgiving Festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday Festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve Festival * Monthly – Monthly Festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Main Hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == annual festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (annual festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} '''Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. It enshrines the spirits of war dead from Saitama Prefecture and was previously designated as a "specified Gokoku Shrine." == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == annual festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} '''Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. It enshrines the spirits of war dead from Saitama Prefecture and was previously designated as a "specified Gokoku Shrine." == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} '''Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. It enshrines the spirits of war dead from Saitama Prefecture and was previously designated as a "specified Gokoku Shrine." == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Original pages]]{{gokoku shrines}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} '''Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. It enshrines the spirits of war dead from Saitama Prefecture and was previously designated as a "specified Gokoku Shrine." == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} '''Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. It enshrines the spirits of war dead from Saitama Prefecture and was previously designated as a "specified Gokoku Shrine." == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Original pages]]{{gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[Category:dd]][[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Original pages]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:bump]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == *
