{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:kk]] PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:from everydodywiki with needed marking of original title]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:From Everybodywiki with needed marking of original title]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]{{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama Gokoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }}[[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{DEFAULTSORT:Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saitama
