revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王|12=draft}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|12=draft}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|12=draft}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] {{citation needed|Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|12=draft|12=draft}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]]

In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Ōmiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]]

{{afc comment|1=see [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} [[Category:Completion tier 7]] {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation revision.comment: 34 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:埼玉縣護國神社 up to rev 99426437
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:03 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 埼玉縣護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px|鳥居]]
鳥居および本殿 |所在地 = [[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]][[高鼻町]]3丁目149番地 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 55 |緯度秒 = 10.6 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 37 |経度秒 = 40.3 |祭神 = 埼玉県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧指定護国神社 |創建 = [[昭和]]9年（[[1934年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |例祭 = [[4月9日]] |地図 = Japan Saitama |ラベル位置 = left }} '''埼玉縣護國神社'''（さいたまけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[埼玉県]][[さいたま市]][[大宮区]]に鎮座する[[護国神社|旧指定護国神社]]である。 == 由緒・歴史 == 埼玉県人の英霊の祭祀は、[[昭和]]以前は県内各所に[[神籬]]を建てて鎮魂慰霊の祭祀を行っていたが、[[満洲事変]]で県下の英霊が増えたのを機に、[[招魂社]]創設の機運が高まり、昭和9年（[[1934年]]）4月9日に[[朝香宮鳩彦王]]（当時[[近衛師団長]]）臨席の下、「埼玉県招魂社」として鎮座した。社殿の設計は[[二本松孝蔵]]。同14年3月に「埼玉県護國神社」と改称して4月に指定護国神社となる。 [[戦後]]の昭和21年（[[1946年]]）2月に[[宗教法人]]となり、同23年10月に崇敬者の総意により一時「埼霊神社」と改称したが、同27年に「埼玉県護國神社」に復した。昭和30年（1955年）、奉賛会が設立される。 [[1967年]]（昭和42年）[[10月23日]]、[[昭和天皇]]、[[香淳皇后]]が[[第22回国民体育大会]]に合わせて県内を行幸啓。護国神社が訪問先の一つとなる{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=131 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。 また、平成25年10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == [[鳥羽・伏見の戦い]]以後の国事に殉じた埼玉県関係の戦没者51180[[柱]]を祀る。 == 祭事 == * 1月1日 - 元旦祭 * 2月11日 - 紀元祭 * 2月17日 - 祈年祭 * 春分の日 - 春分祭 * 4月9日 - 例大祭 * 5月5日 - 祝子祭 * 8月15日 - みたま祭り * 秋分の日 - 秋分祭 * 11月3日 - 文化祭 * 11月23日 - 新嘗祭・勤労感謝祭 * 12月23日 - 天長祭 * 12月31日 - 除夜祭 * 毎月1日 - 月次祭 == ギャラリー == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|本殿 File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|埼玉県傷痍軍人の塔
第58代[[厚生省|厚生大臣]]　[[渡辺美智雄]]　書 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク== *[https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ 埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会] {{DEFAULTSORT:さいたまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:さいたま市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:大宮区の歴史]] [[Category:大宮区の建築物]] {{shinto-stub}}

第58代[[厚生省|厚生大臣]]　[[渡辺美智雄]]　書 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク== *[https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ 埼玉県護国神社清掃奉仕の会] {{DEFAULTSORT:さいたまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:さいたま市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:大宮区の歴史]] [[Category:大宮区の建築物]] {{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{citation needed|Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]]

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{citation needed|Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]]

The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Showa|qq=}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōjun|qq=}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * (Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince {{ill|Yasuhiko Asaka|ja|朝香宮鳩彦王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasuhiko Asaka|qq=}}, who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Showa|qq=}} and {{ill|Empress Kōjun|ja|香淳皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōjun|qq=}} visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:さいたま市の神社]] [[Category:大宮区の建築物]] [[Category:大宮区の歴史]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}}
