Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}}

(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}}

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[World War II]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11427145]] Wikidata name: Saitama-ken Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Everybody linked]][[every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[sn:aitama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11427145]] Wikidata name: Saitama-ken Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Saitama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11427145}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11427145]] Wikidata name: Saitama-ken Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Saitama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11427145}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11427145]] Wikidata name: Saitama-ken Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Saitama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11427145}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == [[sn:commons:Category:Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11427145]] Wikidata name: Saitama-ken Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Saitama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Saitama Gokoku Shrine|Saitama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 埼玉縣護國神社 | image = [[File:Gateway of shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|250px]] | caption = Torii and main hall of Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|55|10.6|N|139|37|40.3|E|region:JP-11_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-149 Takahana-cho, Ōmiya-ku, Saitama City, Saitama Prefecture, Japan | deity = War dead from Saitama Prefecture | established = [[:en:1934|1934]] | architecture = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = April 9 (festival) }} '''Saitama Gokoku Shrine''' (埼玉縣護國神社, ''Saitama-ken Gokoku Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} located in [[:en:Ōmiya-ku|Ōmiya-ku]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]], Japan. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It was not linked to the Army.{{Cite journal |last=Miyaji |first=Naokazu |last2=Hiyane |first2=Antei |date=1966 |title=An Interview with Dr. Naokazu Miyaji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232990 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=143–153 |issn=0010-7557}} == History == Before the shrine's establishment, spirits of Saitama residents who died in national service were commemorated at various locations within the prefecture. However, the growing number of fallen soldiers during the [[:en:Manchurian Incident|Manchurian Incident]] heightened the need for a central place of worship. On April 9, 1934, the shrine was founded as the "Saitama Prefecture Shōkonsha" with the presence of Prince [[:en:Prince Yasuhiko Asaka|Yasuhiko Asaka]], who was then commander of the Imperial Guards. The shrine's architectural design was created by {{ill|Kōzō Nihonmatsu|ja|二本松孝蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzō Nihonmatsu|qq=}}. In 1939, it was renamed "Saitama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine" and officially designated a Gokoku Shrine in April of that year. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1946. It briefly changed its name to "Sairei Shrine" in 1948 before reverting to its original name in 1952 following the consensus of its devotees. The shrine gained further recognition in 1955 with the establishment of its support organization. In 1967, during the [[22nd National Sports festival of Japan]], [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kōjun]] visited the shrine as part of their Saitama tour.{{Cite book|author=Harutake Hara|title=The Complete Records of Emperor Showa's Trains|publisher=Shinchōsha|year=2016|page=131|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}} In 2013, a statue honoring Special Attack Forces (''Tokkō Yūshi'') was erected within the precincts. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates 51,180 spirits of individuals from Saitama Prefecture who perished in national service from the [[:en:Battle of Toba–Fushimi|Battle of Toba–Fushimi]] onward. == festivals == * January 1 – New Year's Day festival * February 11 – National Foundation Day festival * February 17 – Prayer festival * Vernal Equinox Day – Spring Equinox festival * April 9 – annual Grand festival * May 5 – Children's Day festival * August 15 – ''Mitama Matsuri'' (festival of Souls) * Autumnal Equinox Day – Autumn Equinox festival * November 3 – Culture Day festival * November 23 – Niiname festival & Labor Thanksgiving festival * December 23 – Emperor's Birthday festival * December 31 – New Year's Eve festival * Monthly – Monthly festival on the 1st == Gallery == File:Shrine for the departed soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|main hall File:The monument of the disabled soldier who was born in Saitama.jpg|Monument to Disabled Soldiers from Saitama Prefecture, inscribed by Watanabe Michio, former Minister of Health and Welfare == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.facebook.com/saigokai/ Saitama Gokoku Shrine Cleaning Volunteer Group] (Official Facebook) {{shinto-stub}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|埼玉縣護國神社|version=99426437|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Saitama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:埼玉縣護國神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama, Saitama]] [[Category:Buildings in Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:History of Omiya-ku, Saitama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Saitamaken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11427145}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
